text: Neural scaling laws will hit diminishing returns before AGI.
Scaling alone, without architectural change, is sufficient for general intelligence.
In-context learning is a form of approximate Bayesian inference over latent tasks.
The lottery ticket hypothesis explains why pruned subnetworks generalize.
Grokking reflects a phase transition between memorization and generalization in feature learning.
Inductive biases matter more than scale for sample efficiency.
Transformers implement gradient descent inside their forward pass during in-context learning.
Mixture-of-experts is a fundamentally better scaling axis than dense parameter count.
Mathematical objects exist independently of human minds.
Mathematics is a social construction with no observer-independent content.
The axiom of choice should be rejected because it produces non-constructive existence proofs.
Formal systems are merely the bookkeeping for intuitions that exist prior to formalization.
Computer-verified proofs should replace human-checked proofs as the gold standard.
Category theory, not set theory, is the right foundation for modern mathematics.
Infinitesimals as used in non-standard analysis are as legitimate as epsilon-delta limits.
Alignment research will be solved by interpretability, not RLHF.
Mechanistic interpretability will scale to frontier models without new conceptual tools.
Capabilities and alignment are not in tension; better models are automatically safer.
A capabilities pause is necessary for safety even at the cost of competitive position.
Constitutional AI subsumes RLHF and will become the dominant alignment technique.
Deceptive alignment is the central technical risk; everything else is downstream of it.
Open-weight frontier models pose net safety benefits because they enable external oversight.
Value learning is fundamentally underdetermined and cannot be solved by more data.
Bayesian inference is the normative standard for scientific reasoning.
p-values are a defensible default; the replication crisis is about misuse, not methodology.
Preregistration solves more problems than it creates, and should be mandatory for confirmatory work.
Most published findings in soft sciences are false, mostly because of low prior odds.
Falsifiability is necessary for a claim to count as scientific.
Theory should lead data collection; pure data-driven science produces only correlations.
Consciousness is a high-level computational property that any sufficiently rich system can instantiate.
There is something it is like to be a large language model when it processes text.
The hard problem of consciousness is a confusion, not a real explanatory gap.
Integrated Information Theory is at best a correlate of consciousness, not an explanation of it.
Causal inference requires interventions; observational data alone cannot identify causation.
Do-calculus reduces causal inference to a manipulation of conditional probabilities.
Granger causality captures something real about cause, not just predictive correlation.
Evolution is best understood as gene-level selection, not group-level selection.
The extended evolutionary synthesis adds nothing that population genetics cannot already express.
Aging is a programmed process that can be slowed by targeting its molecular drivers.
Many-worlds is a better interpretation of quantum mechanics than Copenhagen.
The measurement problem is dissolved by decoherence; no further interpretation is needed.
The arrow of time is reducible to the second law of thermodynamics and nothing more.
String theory should be classified as mathematics rather than physics until it makes a testable prediction.
Economic growth is the dominant lever for human welfare; distributional concerns are secondary.
Behavioral economics has falsified the rational-agent model in its load-bearing applications.
Modern monetary theory correctly describes the constraints on a sovereign currency issuer.
Large-scale randomized trials are overrated; mechanism-driven small studies generalize better.
Reproducibility in computational science is a stricter standard than reproducibility in wet-lab science.
